transcript: Después de 10 años como maestra de primaria, aquí están las cosas que ya no me importan. Número uno, tarea. Los niños van a ir a casa y lo hacen por su cuenta, lo hacen con mucho apoyo de adultos o se van a casa y no lo hacen todo y vigilar eso es una especie de dolor en la parte trasera. Preferiría que simplemente se fueran a casa, pasar tiempo con su familia, salir afuera, correr por ahí, tener una buena cena, o como mínimo, leer durante 20 minutos y estadísticamente hablando, no hay datos que apoyen que la tarea realmente mejora el rendimiento académico de todos modos. Número dos, aperitivos en clase. Todos mis estudiantes saben que tengo una cubierta de snack completamente surtida lista para ellos siempre que lo necesiten o pueden traer su propio snack y si pueden comerlo y no hacer un desastre o limpiar un desastre si hay uno y no distraer a esos alrededor de ellos sigan adelante y coman yo merienda durante todo el día para que me vean haciendo eso y no se les permite, es un poco injusto. Número tres, usando el baño. Ahora, tengo un baño muy bonito. Tengo la suerte de tener un baño en mi clase, pero no hago que mis estudiantes me escriban una nota o me den algún tipo de señal de mano loca. Si el deber llama, son alumnos de quinto grado. Levántate en silencio. No interrumpas a los que te rodean. Entra ahí. Cuida los negocios, sal, lávate las manos, vuelve a sentarte y vuelve a trabajar. Y número cuatro, cuando los estudiantes me llaman hermano, amigo o hermano. No me ofende en absoluto. Así que si no me ofende, no se ofendan por mi. ¿Voy a hablar formalmente así a mi jefe o a alguien súper importante? Absolutamente no. title: Post by soulmindhub
description:
Neuroscience distinguishes raw cognitive ability from metacognition—the capacity to monitor, evaluate, and regulate one’s own thoughts and emotions. This self-reflective process engages higher-order prefrontal networks involved in monitoring and control, allowing individuals to interrupt automatic reactions and update beliefs based on new information.

Functional imaging studies show that observing and labeling internal states (“I’m anxious,” “I’m angry”) increases activity in regulatory prefrontal regions while reducing reactivity in limbic areas such as the amygdala. This shift supports emotional regulation, reduced impulsivity, and improved decision-making.

Memory research further indicates that when experiences are recalled with conscious awareness, they enter a reconsolidation window—during which emotional intensity can diminish and interpretations can be modified. Over time, repeated self-observation and labeling reshape neural signaling patterns through plasticity.

Metacognition is not motivational rhetoric. It reflects measurable changes in brain activity associated with learning, emotional control, and adaptive behavior. Growth occurs not by suppressing thoughts, but by noticing them—creating the distance needed for the brain to update itself.

title: Post by soulmindhub
description:
Otto Warburg (1883–1970) - A Nobel Prize winning biochemist who reshaped how science understands cellular energy.

In 1931, Warburg received the Nobel Prize for uncovering how cells generate energy — pioneering the field of cellular metabolism. His work later described what became known as the Warburg Effect: the observation that many cancer cells rely heavily on glucose fermentation rather than efficient oxygen-based respiration.

Warburg was not a fringe thinker. He was one of the most influential scientists of the 20th century, nominated for the Nobel Prize dozens of times and deeply respected by his peers. His ideas sparked decades of research into metabolism, mitochondria, and disease — debates that continue in modern science today.

His legacy lives on wherever researchers ask a fundamental question: What happens when the way a cell makes energy goes wrong?

Science evolves. Questions remain. Curiosity drives progress.

title: Post by the.alchemist
description:
Metacognition is one of those words that sounds clinical until you realize what it actually means. It's the ability to observe your own mind while it's operating, to watch a thought arise and recognize it as a thought rather than reality.

Researchers at University College London found that people with higher metacognitive ability have more gray matter in the anterior prefrontal cortex, the part of the brain responsible for self-reflection and decision-making.

 This isn't just a personality trait you're born with or without. Studies show metacognition can be developed through deliberate practice, meaning the witness that's been inside you this whole time can be strengthened like a muscle. 

Every wisdom tradition has pointed to this capacity in its own language, but now we have the science to back it up. The question is whether you'll keep living inside your thoughts or finally step back and meet the part of you that's been watching all along.

Dr. Johanna Budwig was a German biochemist with doctorates in physics and chemistry. She worked as Chief Expert for pharmaceuticals and fats at the German Federal Health Office.

In the 1950s, she discovered something that threatened everything the food and pharmaceutical industries were built on.

She found that people with cancer were consistently deficient in polyunsaturated fatty acids. Without these fats, cell membranes break down. Oxygen can't enter cells. And when cells lose oxygen, cancer thrives.

Her solution was almost too simple. Flaxseed oil mixed with cottage cheese. The sulfhydryl proteins in cottage cheese bond with omega-3s in flaxseed oil, making them absorbable by cells. This floods cells with oxygen.

She reported extraordinary results across multiple cancer types. Studies on flaxseed have since confirmed its ability to reduce tumor growth, cut metastasis by up to 70%, and enhance the effects of existing cancer treatments.

So why haven't you heard of her?

Because she refused to sell her protocol to pharmaceutical companies who wanted to patent it and profit from it. She openly attacked trans fats, processed oils, and margarine decades before the mainstream caught on.

She was blackballed. Silenced. Erased from medical history.

She died in 2003 at the age of 94. Her work lives on through those who share it.

title: Video by cinesospeso
description:
Osgood Perkins has officially cemented his status as the architect of modern atmospheric horror with Keeper (2025). Collaborating again with cinematographer Jeremy Cox, Perkins utilizes suffocating negative space and deliberate, voyeuristic framing to transform a simple cabin retreat into a claustrophobic nightmare. The terror here is purely architectural; the camera lingers on empty corners and open doorways, forcing the audience to actively scan the frame for threats that may not even exist.
Tatiana Maslany delivers a career-defining performance of unraveling sanity, grounding the folk-horror elements in a terrifyingly tangible emotional reality. While the screenplay by Nick Lepard builds a foundation of relationship toxicity, it is Perkins’ precise control over tone and sound design—specifically Edo Van Breemen’s subliminal score—that instills a physical sense of dread.This film proves that true horror lies not in the monster, but in the silence before it appears.

Follow @cinesospeso for cinema analysis.

title: Video by cristophergeilermd
description:
🧬Your body already makes its own longevity molecule. After 40, you’re running out of it. 🧬Alpha-ketoglutarate (AKG) is a metabolite your mitochondria produce as part of the Krebs cycle. It’s not a drug. It’s not synthetic. But its mechanism of action on aging biology is extraordinary.Here’s what it does simultaneously:
🔴 Inhibits mTOR - the chronic activation of this growth-signaling pathway is one of the primary drivers of cellular aging. AKG puts the brakes on it.
🔵 Rewrites your epigenome - AKG is a required cofactor for TET demethylases and histone demethylases (KDMs), the enzymes that regulate which genes get silenced as you age. It directly modulates H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 marks.
🟢 Mimics caloric restriction - AKG levels naturally rise with fasting, exercise, and dietary restriction. In a landmark 2020 study in Cell Metabolism, aging mice given supplemental calcium-AKG lived longer, showed significantly less frailty, had lower systemic inflammatory cytokines, and demonstrated what the authors called a compression of morbidity - more life, but critically, healthier life. 🐭. The ABLE Trial - a double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT currently underway - is now testing 1g of sustained-release Ca-AKG versus placebo in 120 adults aged 40-60, with DNA methylation age as the primary endpoint. 📚 References:Asadi Shahmirzadi A, et al. Alpha-Ketoglutarate, an Endogenous Metabolite, Extends Lifespan and Compresses Morbidity in Aging Mice. Cell Metabolism. 2020;32(3):447-456. doi:10.1016/j.cmet.2020.08.004Chin RM, et al. The metabolite α-ketoglutarate extends lifespan by inhibiting ATP synthase and TOR. Nature. 2014;510:397-401. doi:10.1038/nature13264

title: Video by judgysociety
description:
🧮

Kaprekar's constant is the number 6174, a four-digit number that emerges when you repeatedly apply a specific process: take any four-digit number (with at least two different digits), rearrange its digits to form the largest and smallest possible numbers, and subtract the smaller from the larger, always ensuring four digits (using leading zeros if needed). This "Kaprekar's routine" always converges to 6174 within seven iterations, after which the process yields 6174 indefinitely (e.g., 7641 - 1467 = 6174).

description:
Caligula (1979) is one of the most controversial films ever made, blending historical drama with shocking explicit content. Inspired by the real-life Roman emperor Caligula, the movie follows his rise to power and his descent into madness, cruelty, and extreme obsession.

The film depicts ancient Rome as a place of absolute power, corruption, violence, and unchecked desire. Caligula’s rule is shown as brutal and chaotic, where human lives, loyalty, and morals mean nothing. As emperor, he indulges in sadistic pleasures, manipulation, and public humiliation, slowly destroying everyone around him—including himself.

What makes Caligula infamous is not just its story, but its explicit scenes, which caused massive controversy worldwide. Many versions of the film were banned, censored, or edited in several countries. Despite this, the movie gained cult status for its bold themes, disturbing imagery, and portrayal of how power without limits can turn a man into a monster.

Caligula is not an easy watch. It’s disturbing, uncomfortable, and extreme—but for many, it remains a symbol of cinema without boundaries, showing the darkest side of human desire and authority.

title: Video by musicallyproper
description:
In 1982, Conrad Schnitzler—once a member of Tangerine Dream and Kluster—dropped ‘The Shark Eats Ice.’ A track so icy, mechanical, and satirical it feels like electroclash 20 years early.

Schnitzler came from Berlin’s art-school avant-garde, where Dada thrived on absurd, vivid phrases that stripped words of rational meaning. 

Born during WWI, Dada was an anti-art movement that embraced nonsense and provocation as a way to reject logic and convention. 

‘The Shark Eats Ice’ feels like exactly that—a machine-beat predator devouring something cold and empty, a surreal title that mirrors the track’s satire of consumer electronics and the dehumanizing chill of machine culture.

(Visuals by the synth performance art pioneers, Tik and Tok)

Your brain works the same way. Your subconscious attention acts as the observer, constantly collapsing one version of reality out of many possible ones. When your focus is trained on threat, lack, or resistance, that’s the timeline that keeps rendering.

You’re not manifesting incorrectly. You’re observing consistently. Change the observer, and the reality being rendered has no choice but to change with it.

title: Video by sheturnedsavage
description:
No one talks about this part.

When pain has been your shadow for years,
it stops feeling like something you carry —
and starts feeling like who you are.

You’ve built yourself around it.
Learned how to function with it.
Learned how to explain yourself through it.

The sadness became your language.
The anxiety became your rhythm.
The grief became your identity.

So when someone says, “You’re healing,”
instead of relief… there’s fear.

Who am I without the ache?
Who am I without the story that shaped me?
Who am I without the battles that carved themselves into my bones?

Healing sounds beautiful in theory.
But in reality?
It feels like walking into a quiet room after years of chaos.

And the quiet is unfamiliar.
The quiet is uncomfortable.
The quiet asks you to meet yourself without the armor.

You are not strange for being scared of that.
You are not weak for hesitating at the edge of peace.

When pain has been your compass,
letting it go can feel like losing direction.

But here’s the truth:

You are not your suffering.
You are not the worst thing that happened to you.
You are not the grief that shaped you.

You are the person who survived it.

And the version of you that exists beyond the pain?
She isn’t empty.
She isn’t lost.
She isn’t unrecognisable.

She’s softer.
Stronger.
Quieter in the best way.

It’s okay to be scared of meeting her.

Just don’t run from her.

title: Video by soulfulsongs77
description:
The way he stopped playing guitar just to hear them sing was priceless✨

“More Than Words” by Extreme was released in 1990 as the third single from their second studio album Pornograffitti. The song became the band’s biggest commercial success, reaching top hit and gaining worldwide popularity for its stripped-down acoustic style, which contrasted sharply with the band’s usual hard rock sound. 

Musically, the track is built around gentle acoustic guitar fingerpicking by Nuno Bettencourt and soft, intimate vocals from Gary Cherone, creating a minimalist arrangement that places emotional emphasis on the message. 

Lyrically, the song explores the idea that saying “I love you” is not enough without meaningful actions to support those words; it challenges superficial expressions of love and calls for sincerity and commitment. 

Its heartfelt delivery and simplicity helped it become one of the most recognizable acoustic ballads of the early 1990s

title: Video by stelle.world
description:
The phrase highest form of intelligence isn't IQ sets the tone for a thought-provoking discussion. According to neuroscientists, this intelligence is something entirely different, and it's rare to find people who possess it. The conversation revolves around metacognition, the ability to think about one's own thinking.

This video delves into the concept of metacognition, explaining that it's the ability to observe one's mind in real time, noticing thoughts, questioning reactions, and interrupting emotional reflexes. The conversation highlights the importance of updating beliefs instead of defending them, and how this process can lead to changes in the brain. The anterior prefrontal cortex, a region responsible for self-observation, activates when one observes their own thoughts. The video also displays on-screen text, highest update beliefs interrupting emotions that can edit its own, which reinforces the idea that metacognition allows for a deeper understanding of one's thoughts and emotions. By exploring this concept, the video provides insight into the workings of the human brain and the potential for personal growth.

The idea that metacognition can be developed and strengthened over time raises interesting questions about personal development and intelligence. As the conversation suggests, quietly outgrowing others around them, those who develop metacognition can experience significant personal growth. What does it mean to truly think about one's own thinking, and how can this ability be cultivated in daily life? The video invites viewers to consider the role of metacognition in their own lives, and to reflect on the potential benefits of developing this rare form of intelligence.
